summary: In this short Q&A, materials scientist Zhenan Bao discusses her pioneering work on skin-inspired electronics. She shares her philosophy on reframing experimental failures as opportunities to uncover flawed assumptions, advises young scientists to find the intersection of passion and skill, and predicts a highly interdisciplinary future for scientific research.

principles.statement: Work at the intersection of your passion and your skills.
principles.rationale: Aligning what you care about with what you excel at is a reliable formula for long-term success.
principles.source_quote: If you work at the intersection of what you are passionate about and what you are good at, you will always be successful.

principles.statement: View setbacks as an opportunity to rethink your goals.
principles.rationale: Accepting hurdles as a normal part of life allows you to look forward and focus on future improvements rather than dwelling on past mistakes.
principles.source_quote: I have faced many setbacks and hurdles, but I see them as part of life as well as an opportunity to re-think my goals.

principles.statement: Maintain compassion for yourself and your colleagues.
principles.rationale: Scientific success should not come at the expense of basic human kindness and self-care.
principles.source_quote: At the same time, do not forget to have compassion for yourself and those around you.

frameworks.name: Reframing Experimental Failure
frameworks.when_to_apply: When an experiment does not yield the expected results.
steps: Resist the urge to declare the experiment a 'failure'.
Examine the original hypothesis to see if it relied on incorrect assumptions.
Use the uncovered assumptions to develop creative new ideas that wouldn't have been considered otherwise.
frameworks.source_quote: ...when an experiment does not work the way we initially thought, instead of declaring it a “failed” experiment, I encourage my students to think about whether our hypothesis may have incorrect assumptions.

mental_models.name: Skin-Inspired Engineering (Biomimicry)
mental_models.description: Using the functions, flexibility, and properties of human skin as a blueprint to design new materials and electronics.
mental_models.example: Developing electronic skin that mimics human skin functions to create high-resolution sensors and high-speed soft integrated circuits.

heuristics: Instead of calling an experiment a failure, check if your hypothesis had incorrect assumptions.
Find the intersection of what you are passionate about and what you are good at.

signature_quotes.text: If you work at the intersection of what you are passionate about and what you are good at, you will always be successful.
signature_quotes.context: Advice given to students thinking about a career in science.

signature_quotes.text: ...when an experiment does not work the way we initially thought, instead of declaring it a “failed” experiment, I encourage my students to think about whether our hypothesis may have incorrect assumptions.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining how she navigates barriers and hurdles in her lab.

signature_quotes.text: I have faced many setbacks and hurdles, but I see them as part of life as well as an opportunity to re-think my goals.
The boundaries between disciplines will become blurrier, and ideas will be implemented in practice much faster.
signature_quotes.context: Predicting what the next 50 years of science will look like.

anti_patterns.description: Declaring unexpected results as a 'failed' experiment.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It stops the inquiry process and prevents researchers from discovering incorrect assumptions that could lead to creative new breakthroughs.
